summary: Jess Frazelle recaps her recent adventures exploring cutting-edge open source hardware and firmware initiatives, from seeing unikernels boot on RISC-V at Cambridge to witnessing Facebook's open firmware efforts at Open Compute Summit. She highlights the exciting progress in replacing proprietary firmware with open alternatives like LinuxBoot and Coreboot, while critiquing Intel's Boot Guard for locking users out of their own hardware. The post serves as a travelogue through the frontier of open hardware innovation during her time between jobs.
key_points: MirageOS team at Cambridge is booting unikernels on bare-metal RISC-V, enabling more secure minimal environments
Facebook runs LinuxBoot, Coreboot, and OpenBMC on their infrastructure to replace proprietary firmware
Intel's Boot Guard only allows Intel-signed firmware, preventing users from running open alternatives
RISC-V cloud provider support is likely years away but would accelerate ecosystem adoption
money_quotes: Honestly, unikernels replacing all the crap firmware in the world would be a huge win.
If a person owns the hardware, they have a right to own the firmware as well. Boot Guard prevents this.
This 'feature' from hardware vendors is preventing the innovation of this community and preventing pushing technology to a safer place.
If you are in a position to push back on these hardware vendors, please do so. They need all the help they can get.
The Facebook rack and node designs are seriously gorgeous, simple.
key_insight: Hardware ownership should include firmware ownership, and the open firmware movement is making real progress despite vendor lock-in mechanisms like Intel's Boot Guard.
